title: Chapter 21: The 50M Cliff
content: Twenty-six million two days ago.
Thirty-one million yesterday morning.
Thirty-five by lunch.
Forty before I could blink.

The number on my phone screen burns. Not metaphorically — the thing is running hot, the display cranked to full brightness like it's trying to sear the digits into my retinas. 40,217,893. I watch it tick. 40,217,894. 40,217,901. 40,217,943.
That's not growth. That's flooding.

I'm sitting on the kitchen floor because my legs decided they were done with standing. The tile is cold through my jeans. The phone is hot in my hand. My mouth tastes like pennies — adrenaline and brass coating my tongue, that metallic tang you get when your body knows something your brain hasn't caught up to yet.
The follower count ticks again. 40,218,207. Two hundred new followers in the time it took me to breathe.
Two days ago I had twenty-six million followers and a plan. Now I have forty million and a bomb.

Leo's laptop hums from the kitchen table — a steady, low-frequency drone that I feel in my sternum more than hear. He's been running queries since 6 AM. I can hear his fingers on the keyboard. Fast. Angry. The keystrokes have a rhythm like a heartbeat that keeps speeding up.
"Chloe."
I look up. He's turned his screen toward me. Charts. Spikes. A heatmap that's almost entirely red.
"The new followers are shells," he says.
I already know. I've known since the count jumped four million in six hours. Nobody goes viral like that. Nobody.
But I need him to say it.
"What am I looking at?"

He points at the heatmap. "Empty accounts. Zero posting history. Zero followers of their own. Profile pics generated by AI — you can tell because they all have the same slight blur around the left eye. Created in bursts." He taps. A column of timestamps. "Fifty thousand accounts spun up at 2:14 AM. Another fifty thousand at 2:47. Another at 3:03. All from the same server cluster. AWS us-east-1, same availability zone."
"He's not waiting," I say.
"No."
"He's buying my milestones."
Leo's jaw tightens. "He's accelerating. The love deduction could hit any—"
"I know."

I do know. I've known since the number crossed thirty and I felt something shift behind my ribs. Not gone. Not yet. But thinner. Like the walls of a room closing in from the inside. The space where love lives — where it used to live, thick and warm and certain — is getting hollow.
Forty million. Ten to go.
I pull up Evelyn's contact. My thumb hovers over the call button for half a second. Then I press it.
She picks up on the second ring. "Chloe."
"How fast can you file?"

Her voice is clipped. Professional. The voice she uses with clients she's worried about. "Emergency injunction takes seventy-two hours minimum. We need to file in the Southern District, serve the defendant, establish standing—"
"I don't have seventy-two hours, Evelyn."
Silence.

"I'm at forty-two million and climbing." I didn't even look at the phone. I can feel the number. It's in the air, a pressure against my skin. Forty-two and change. Growing while I stand here. "He's bots. He's flooding me with bots. I'm going to hit fifty before you can even get the paperwork stamped."
Another silence. Longer this time.
"I'll push for an emergency hearing," she says. "But Chloe — if the deduction processes before we file, you lose the capacity to be a plaintiff with standing."
"What does that mean?"

"It means you need to be able to demonstrate harm. You need to testify that something was taken from you. If you can't feel love—" She stops. Starts again, slower. "If you can't feel love, you can't testify that it was taken. The court will see a plaintiff who can't articulate her own injury. Mephisto's lawyers will argue you have no damages because you can't experience the absence. They'll say you're whole."
The kitchen is cold. The electricity in the air — static, sharp, like before a storm — prickles along my forearms. I taste brass again, thick on the back of my tongue.
"So I have to still be damaged to sue for damages."
"Yes."
"But the damage will destroy my ability to sue."
"Yes."
"That's the most disgusting thing I've ever heard."
"It's the law."
Forty-two million. The phone screen burns. The number ticks.
"Seventy-two hours," I say. "That's not going to work."

"I know. I'm moving as fast as I can. But the Southern District doesn't care about follower counts. They care about filing correctly. One procedural error and Mephisto's team gets it dismissed on a technicality."
"Then don't make errors."
"I don't intend to. But I need time, Chloe. And you need to buy me some."
I hang up. I stare at the phone. 42,613,004.
Leo's voice cuts through the static in my skull. "What if I slow the count?"
I look up. He's leaning back in his chair, one hand on his laptop, the other rubbing the back of his neck. His eyes are bloodshot. He's been awake for — I don't know how long. Too long.
"What do you mean?"

"The follower-count API. It queries the database and returns a number. If I can inject false data into the response — make it register fewer followers than it actually has—" He trails off, thinking. "It's not hacking the underlying system. The real count still exists on Mephisto's servers. But the app, the public-facing metrics, your dashboard — those all pull from the API. I can spoof the response."
"Cosmetic."
"Cosmetic," he agrees. "But if it buys us forty-eight hours—"
"It's a band-aid on a hemorrhage."
"It's a band-aid on a hemorrhage while the ambulance is en route." He meets my eyes. "It might be enough."
The laptop hums. The phone burns. The number ticks.
"Do it," I say.

Leo turns back to his screen. His fingers find the keyboard and something shifts in his posture — he hunches forward, shoulders locked, neck bent, and he starts to code. I've seen him code before. Late nights, lazy afternoons, the casual tap-tap-tap of someone doing something they're good at. This is different. This is violent. His fingers move like they're trying to break the keys. The screen scrolls so fast I can't track it. He's not writing code. He's bleeding it.

I watch from the floor. The cold tile seeps through my jeans. The phone screen sears my palm. I can hear Leo's breathing — fast, shallow, focused — and beneath it the steady drone of the laptop's fan, that low hum vibrating in my chest like a second heartbeat.
Forty-three million.

He works for an hour. Two. I lose track. The kitchen gets darker because neither of us gets up to turn on a light. The only illumination is the glow of two screens — his laptop, my phone — casting our faces in pale blue.

At some point I drag myself off the floor and sit on one of the kitchen stools. I watch his code scroll. I don't understand most of it. Python, I think. Requests to some API endpoint I've never heard of. Spoofed headers. Falsified response payloads.
"It's running," he says finally. His voice is hoarse.
I look at my phone.

Incrementing. But slower. Where before I'd see jumps of hundreds, now it's crawling. Ones and twos. Leo's code is intercepting the API responses and — I don't know, doing math, shaving off the bot surges, presenting a cleaner number to the dashboard.
"It's working," I say.

"Don't celebrate." He's squinting at a terminal window. "The bot creation rate is still accelerating. My filter is just intercepting the display. The real count is still climbing. The gap between what your app shows and what Mephisto's servers know — that gap grows every minute. Eventually something's going to notice."
"How long?"
"I don't know. A day? Maybe two if I'm lucky and Mephisto's not paying attention."
Mephisto is always paying attention.

I sit with that for a moment. The brass taste in my mouth. The cold electric air. The hum of the laptop. The burn of the phone screen. All of it pressing in. The kitchen feels smaller than it did this morning. The walls are closer. The ceiling is lower. Everything is contracting toward the number.
Forty-four million by midnight.
Then forty-five.

Leo's code is still running but the bots are faster now. I can see it happening in real time — his filter snags a thousand fake followers, blocks them from the count, and two thousand more pour through before he can adjust. It's like trying to bail out a boat with a coffee cup while someone on the shore keeps pouring water in.

He's sweating. The laptop fan is screaming — not humming anymore, screaming, a high thin whine that burrows into my eardrums. His fingers never stop. Code, deploy, test, adjust. Code, deploy, test, adjust. A desperate loop. The kind of rhythm you fall into when stopping means dying.
"Leo."
"I know." He doesn't look up.
"How bad is it?"

"The app's authentication system is detecting my interference." He gestures at a log I can't read. "It's adapting. Every time I close a vector, it finds another one. It's — it's learning. Or someone's reconfiguring it on the other end."
The system is fighting back.
My phone buzzes. A notification slides down from the top of the screen:
[Anomaly detected in Creator metric reporting. Diagnostic running.]
My stomach drops.
"It knows," I say.
Leo looks at the notification. His face goes gray. "That's an automated flag. Not human. Not yet. But if the diagnostic confirms interference—"
"How long before someone sees it?"
"Depends on who's monitoring. If it's just routine QA, maybe hours. If Mephisto has alerts set for your account specifically—"
He doesn't finish. He doesn't have to.

Forty-five million. My phone says forty-five but the real number is higher. Leo's filter is buying us time, yes, but the gap is a debt and debts come due. The real count could be forty-six. Forty-seven. I don't know. I can't know. I'm driving blind toward a cliff and someone fogged the windshield.

I pace. Three steps to the fridge. Three steps back. The cold electricity in the air makes my arms prickle. I taste brass and something worse underneath — fear, maybe, or what's left of it. The space behind my ribs is quieter than it should be. Not silent. Not yet. But the ambient emotional noise — the low hum of caring about things, the warmth of attachment, the pull of connection — that hum is getting faint.
I try to think about my mother. There should be something. A twist. A pang. The specific ache of loving someone complicated.
Nothing. Not nothing — thinning. Like a radio signal losing strength. The station is still broadcasting. I just can't hear it as clearly.
Forty-six million.
I call Evelyn again. It's 2 AM. She picks up on the first ring. She hasn't been sleeping either.
"Update," I say. "I'm at forty-six. Maybe more — Leo's running interference but it's cosmetic. The real number is higher."
"How much higher?"
"I don't know. Maybe forty-seven. Maybe forty-eight."

A sharp intake of breath. "I'm drafting the complaint now. It's not finished. The jurisdiction argument is solid but the evidence package is incomplete. If we file tonight, it's half the case we could have by Thursday."
"File tonight."
"Chloe—"
"Whatever you have. File it. Tonight."
"The judge will—"
"I don't care about the judge. I care about not waking up tomorrow unable to feel love for my own sister. File. It. Tonight."
Silence. Then: "I'll file by morning. Six AM at the latest."
"Thank you."
I hang up. The phone is hot in my hand. The screen is too bright. The number glows.
Three million to go.

I set the phone on the counter. Screen up. The number pulses like a heartbeat. I can feel it in the air — not the number itself, but what it represents. The countdown. The fuse. The tickticktick of a bomb I can't defuse and can't run from.

Leo is still coding. Hunched over his laptop, fingers flying, fan screaming, the machine running hot enough that I can feel the heat radiating off it from three feet away. He's muttering under his breath. Fragments of code. Curses. Something that sounds like a prayer but probably isn't.
I cross the kitchen.
I don't do it deliberately. My body moves on its own, pulled toward the only source of warmth in a room that's gone cold and electric and wrong. I stand next to him. Close enough that our arms touch.

His arm is warm. That's the thing I register — the only sensory input that feels real right now. The phone is hot but that's a burn, a warning. The air is cold and sharp and prickle. The taste in my mouth is brass and adrenaline. But Leo's arm against mine — that's warm. Human-warm. Alive-warm.

I don't hug him. I don't know how to want to hug him anymore. The impulse is there — somewhere — but it's muffled. Like hearing someone call your name from the other side of a wall. You know they're calling. You just can't make yourself move toward the sound.
He doesn't look up. His eyes are locked on his screen. Code scrolling. Bots multiplying. The number climbing.
But he leans into me. Just slightly. His arm presses against mine with a little more weight. A deliberate shift. He knows I'm there. He knows I need something I can't ask for. He leans in anyway.
That's enough.
That has to be enough.
The phone ticks. 47,102,883. 47,102,901. 47,102,944. The bots don't sleep. Mephisto's algorithm doesn't rest. It's buying my milestones and the price is my soul and the invoice is almost due.

The diagnostic is still running on my phone. The system knows Leo is interfering. The complaint isn't filed yet. Evelyn needs until morning — but morning might be too late. Three million followers. At this rate, hours. Maybe less.
The space behind my ribs is hollowing out. Thin ice over deep water. I can feel it flexing. Ready to crack.
I stand in the dark kitchen with my arm against Leo's and I listen to the bots multiply and I wait for the ice to break.
